next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 3

By the time Mina left the screened walkway, the camp had gone from evening noise to the thinner sounds people made when they were pretending to sleep in heat. A box fan knocked somewhere in the guest rooms, once per rotation, the casing loose enough to complain but not loose enough to stop. The generator kept its low metal headache behind the shed. Beyond the pilings, the dark had weight. It held the channels flat and hidden, and it held the rookery too, spoonbills muttering in the mangroves with the ugly intimate sound of birds settling on eggs and each other.

She had changed into the least reflective clothes she owned, black shorts, dark long-sleeved shirt despite the heat, old deck shoes she did not mind ruining. Bug dope slicked the backs of her knees. She had a red-filter flashlight in one pocket, a small notebook in a zip bag in the other, and no camera worth the shame of carrying. If she came back with nothing, a camera would only turn the whole night into a better joke. If she came back with something, a camera phone clip through mangrove dark would not save her name.

The shell path to the county dock kept snagging at her ankles with roots and broken oyster. Mosquitoes found the strip of skin above her collar and needled there until she slapped one and felt her own blood wet on her fingertip. She kept walking.

Tomas was at the fuel cage under the dock light, half in yellow glare and half out of it. He had a penlight clenched in his teeth and a pair of cheap reading glasses low on his nose while he copied a number from the pump into the district ledge...
open_hooks_to_carry: Mina exhausts official routes for night access and learns every legal path is too slow.
Tomas tests how badly she wants the creek by offering fuel, access, and discretion at a personal price.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ck in a fragment she did not invite: motel ice melting under a cheap cooler, Jonah's voice saying hold it level, hold it level, as if level could save anything. She shut her eyes until the fragment broke apart. What had ruined her once did not stop wanting her. That was the ugliest part. Not the penalty, not the gossip. Wanting it still.

Out in the dark, the airboat moved off. She could hear it by its changing pressure on the night, the engine swallowed and returned by bends in the creek.

Mina opened her eyes.

Imogen could have the daylight version. The clean shirts. The donor voice. The woman on the platform who knew where to point binoculars and how to make retirees feel chosen by a bird. Mina would carry bait, fold linens, smile through the heat, and make herself worth the bunk.

But she was not leaving after a week if the rumor held even half the weight it had taken on in her body. She would stay. She would learn the camp's rhythms, the boardwalk to the county dock, the timing of tides and guest dinners and who locked what where. If Tomas Acosta was the nearest route into the night channels, then he was a route. Married, amused, wary, county-issued, whatever else came attached.

The fan rattled once in the window and nearly stopped. Mina kicked the wall near the floor. It caught again and lurched back into motion.

When the airboat noise had thinned to almost nothing, she was still standing there, listening like she could hear blue through wood and water both. By the time she lay down, the decision had settled in her with the hot, mean steadiness of a hook set clean.
state_deltas_required: Protected-rookery access rules, zero legitimate fuel allotment, and Mina's shrinking contract window
Mina chooses illegal access by entering a transactional affair with Tomas.
